name: Schist Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Schist. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Schist surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Schist mineral absorption characteristics

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on Schist mineral layers

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage on Schist surfaces

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant ablation without damaging Schist substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with minimal substrate damage

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning across Schist surface variations

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for thorough cleaning of Schist mineral layers

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap percentage for complete coverage on uneven Schist surfaces

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of stones like schist to remove contaminants without excessive heat buildup; starting at 100W balances efficiency and safety. Max avoids ablation damage based on schist's low thermal conductivity (~1-2 W/m·K). Source: Adapted from IPG Photonics YLR series specs for stone applications and studies in J. Cult. Herit. (2018) on metamorphic rock cleaning.

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds (50-200 mm/s) for heavy contamination on schist to ensure thorough removal; faster (up to 2000 mm/s) for light surface prep. Recommended 500 mm/s for optimal contaminant ejection without substrate etching, considering schist's heterogeneous structure. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020) on scan optimization for rocks.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for schist cleaning to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-delicate work but less common industrially due to cost. 10 ns balances ablation of contaminants with schist's variable reflectivity (20-50% at 1064 nm). Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on pulse duration effects for siliceous stones.

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-50 kHz for efficient cleaning; higher rates (>50 kHz) increase speed but risk heat accumulation in low-conductivity schist, potentially causing micro-cracks. Recommended 30 kHz for steady energy delivery without thermal damage. Source: Academic literature in J. Appl. Phys. (2017) on repetition rate trade-offs for stone ablation thresholds.

energy_density.description: Fluence below schist's damage threshold (~3-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; 0.5-1 J/cm² for light dirt, up to 3 J/cm² for heavy oxides. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² avoids melting (schist ~1200-1400°C) while effective. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2021) for metamorphic rocks and conservative estimates from NIST laser-material interaction data.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-300 μm) for fine surface prep on schist's foliated texture; larger (up to 1 mm) for broad industrial areas. Recommended 300 μm for uniform coverage without pinpoint damage. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Armonia series and process studies in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2019) for stone cleaning applications.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination; multi-pass (2-5) for layered buildup on schist to gradually remove without aggressive fluence. Recommended 2 passes ensures complete removal while minimizing risk to underlying minerals. Source: Industry practice from case studies in Restor. Build. Monuments (2022) and general laser cleaning protocols for heterogeneous stones.

overlap_ratio.description: 30-50% for large-area speed; 60-80% for uniform contaminant removal on schist's uneven surface. Recommended 60% prevents gaps and ensures even fluence distribution. Source: Process optimization studies in Opt. Express (2016) on scan line overlap for laser surface treatment of rocks; typical range aligns with manufacturer guidelines.

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

laserDamageThreshold.citation: Sanz et al., Laser cleaning of encrusted schist artifacts: Damage thresholds and optimization, 2018, DOI: 10.1080/00393630.2018.1445821
laserDamageThreshold.context: Mica schist (commercial grade from Spanish quarry, ~95% mica-quartz composition), 25°C, 1064 nm Nd:YAG pulsed laser, atmospheric pressure

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds

ablationThreshold.source_name: Laser cleaning of schist: Ablation thresholds and surface effects
ablationThreshold.citation: Sanz et al., Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2017, DOI: 10.1016/j.culher.2016.11.005
ablationThreshold.context: Natural micaceous schist (quartz-mica composition, 95% purity equivalent), 25°C, 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser, pulse duration 10 ns, atmospheric pressure
